title: Point of Care Ultrasound
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: Wilma Chan, Nilam J. Soni, Paul H. Mayo

items.title: Why POCUS is Integral to Bedside Clinical Decision-Making
items.content: POCUS allows clinicians to acquire, interpret, and integrate ultrasonographic views in real time at the patient's bedside. This immediacy enhances diagnostic and therapeutic decisions without replacing traditional imaging specialists or high-resolution modalities like CT or MRI.
keyPoints: Real-time acquisition and interpretation by treating clinician
Improves diagnostic and therapeutic decisions at bedside
Distinct from consultative ultrasound involving separate roles for sonographer, interpreter, and clinician
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · Point-of-Care Ultrasound | DEFINITION

items.title: How POCUS Enhances Internal Medicine Practice
items.content: Since the 2000s, internal medicine has seen growing interest in POCUS due to its ability to enhance diagnostic accuracy, treatment, monitoring, and screening. It also boosts patient and clinician confidence while improving procedural safety.
keyPoints: Expanding use since the 2000s
Enhances diagnostic accuracy, treatment, monitoring, and screening
Improves patient and clinician confidence and procedural safety
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · Point-of-Care Ultrasound | DEFINITION

items.title: Cardiac POCUS for Shock State Categorization
items.content: Focused cardiac point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) is crucial in categorizing shock states by assessing left ventricular systolic function. This assessment helps guide immediate clinical decision-making.
keyPoints: Five core views: parasternal long-axis, parasternal short-axis, apical four-chamber, subcostal four-chamber, and inferior vena cava.
Left ventricular systolic function can be categorized as normal (Video 493-1), hyperdynamic (Video 493-2), moderately reduced (Video 493-3), or severely reduced (Video 493-4).
Immediate management changes with findings like acute right ventricular failure, cardiac tamponade, and severe valvular abnormalities.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · CARDIAC | LUNG AND PLEURA

items.title: Lung Ultrasound for Pneumothorax Detection
items.content: Thoracic ultrasonography is superior to chest x-ray in detecting pneumothorax, early interstitial processes, and small pleural effusions. It provides real-time imaging that can guide clinical decisions.
keyPoints: High-frequency probes image the pleural surface; low-frequency probes penetrate deeper into the thorax.
Ultrasound is superior to chest x-ray for detecting pneumothorax and small pleural effusions.
Normal lung aeration on ultrasound shows lung sliding (Video 493-17) and A-lines (Video 493-18).
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · CARDIAC | LUNG AND PLEURA

items.title: Pleural Effusion Assessment with Ultrasound
items.content: Ultrasound is effective in quantifying pleural effusions and guiding drainage procedures, reducing complications such as pneumothorax.
keyPoints: Pleural fluid appears as a hypoechoic space bounded by the diaphragm, chest wall, and atelectatic lung (Video 493-11).
Effusions are quantified as small (Video 493-12), moderate (Video 493-13), or large (Video 493-14) and assessed as simple, complex nonseptated (Video 493-15), or complex septated (Video 493-16).
Ultrasound guidance for pleural drainage reduces the risk of pneumothorax and bleeding.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · CARDIAC | LUNG AND PLEURA

items.title: POCUS for Peritoneal Free Fluid Evaluation
items.content: Point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) is effective in detecting peritoneal free fluid, identifying a safe site for paracentesis, and avoiding unnecessary procedures when insufficient ascites are present.
keyPoints: POCUS can detect as little as 100–500 mL of peritoneal free fluid.
Improves procedural success and reduces complication rates by identifying a safe site for paracentesis.
Eliminates attempts at paracentesis when an insufficient volume of ascites is present.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · ABDOMEN

items.title: POCUS in Diagnosing Small-Bowel Obstruction (SBO)
items.content: POCUS aids in diagnosing SBO by visualizing fluid-filled small-bowel loops, detecting dilation, and observing peristalsis, facilitating rapid diagnosis and intervention.
keyPoints: Diagnostic criteria for SBO include bowel diameter >2.5 cm and fluid-filled loops with visible plicae circularis.
Hyperactive to-and-fro peristalsis within small-bowel loops is indicative of SBO.
POCUS can expedite early intervention and surgical consultation for new SBO cases.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · ABDOMEN

items.subtopic: LOWER EXTREMITY DEEP-VEIN THROMBOSIS | SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE | VASCULAR ACCESS
items.title: Use of Compression Ultrasound for DVT Diagnosis
items.content: Two-dimensional compression ultrasound is a rapid and accurate method for diagnosing deep-vein thrombosis (DVT). It can be learned quickly by clinicians and provides diagnostic accuracy comparable to traditional duplex or triplex ultrasound exams. The technique involves compressing major venous branchpoints perpendicularly until the vein walls touch, identifying noncompressible veins as indicative of DVT without needing to visualize intraluminal clots.
keyPoints: Two-dimensional compression ultrasound is rapid and accurate for diagnosing DVT.
Comparable diagnostic accuracy to traditional duplex or triplex ultrasound exams.
Performed at major venous branchpoints with perpendicular compression technique.
Noncompressible vein indicates DVT, visualization of intraluminal clot not required.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · LOWER EXTREMITY DEEP-VEIN THROMBOSIS | SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE | VASCULAR ACCESS

items.subtopic: LOWER EXTREMITY DEEP-VEIN THROMBOSIS | SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE | VASCULAR ACCESS
items.title: Benefits of Ultrasound Guidance in Central Venous Catheter Insertion
items.content: Ultrasound guidance significantly enhances the success and safety of central venous catheter (CVC) insertion, particularly for internal jugular CVCs. It improves procedure success rates while reducing complications such as pneumothorax and arterial punctures by allowing preprocedure identification of vessels and real-time needle visualization during insertion.
keyPoints: Ultrasound guidance increases success rates and reduces complications in CVC insertion.
Particularly beneficial for internal jugular CVCs, reducing risks like pneumothorax and arterial puncture.
Preprocedure ultrasound survey identifies potential vessels and reveals venous thrombosis or atypical anatomy.
Real-time visualization during insertion minimizes procedure attempts and needle redirections.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · LOWER EXTREMITY DEEP-VEIN THROMBOSIS | SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE | VASCULAR ACCESS

items.title: Why is there no widely accepted certification for POCUS?
items.content: Point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) lacks a universally recognized certification, leading to variability in training and competence across specialties. While some programs like critical care and emergency medicine require comprehensive POCUS training, internal medicine does not mandate such training at the residency level.
keyPoints: No widely accepted certification for POCUS exists.
Critical care and emergency medicine often require comprehensive POCUS training.
Internal medicine does not require comprehensive POCUS training during residency.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · PATHWAYS | CERTIFICATION | CREDENTIALING AND PRIVILEGES | FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.title: How will AI impact future POCUS use in clinical care?
items.content: Artificial intelligence is expected to enhance both the training and application of point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) in clinical settings. It promises to improve remote patient monitoring, particularly for conditions like heart failure, by facilitating tele-ultrasound capabilities.
keyPoints: AI will facilitate POCUS training and use.
Tele-ultrasound can aid in remote patient care delivery.
Remote serial monitoring of conditions such as heart failure may be enhanced with AI integration.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · PATHWAYS | CERTIFICATION | CREDENTIALING AND PRIVILEGES | FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.question: Which of the following best describes point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS)?
options: The acquisition, interpretation, and clinical integration of ultrasonographic views by a treating clinician in real time at the patient's bedside.
An imaging process where a sonographer acquires images for an imaging specialist to interpret.
A replacement for computed tomography (CT) or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI).
A procedure primarily used to visualize superficial structures with limited penetration.
items.explanation: POCUS involves real-time acquisition and interpretation by the treating clinician at the bedside, distinct from consultative ultrasound.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · Point-of-Care Ultrasound | DEFINITION

items.question: What is a key distinction between POCUS and consultative ultrasound?
options: POCUS uses higher frequency probes than consultative ultrasound.
In POCUS, the treating clinician integrates findings immediately, unlike in consultative ultrasound.
Consultative ultrasound does not involve imaging specialists.
POCUS is only used for trauma care.
items.explanation: The key distinction is that in POCUS, the treating clinician interprets and integrates findings at the bedside immediately.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · Point-of-Care Ultrasound | DEFINITION

items.question: What was a significant development for POCUS in emergency departments during the 1980s?
options: The introduction of handheld ultrasound devices.
The adoption of POCUS as part of trauma care.
items.explanation: POCUS became part of trauma care in emergency departments during the 1980s, marking a significant development.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · Point-of-Care Ultrasound | DEFINITION

items.question: Which of the following views is NOT part of the limited or focused cardiac POCUS exam?
items.explanation: The lateral chest wall view is not part of the core views in a limited or focused cardiac POCUS exam.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · CARDIAC | LUNG AND PLEURA

items.question: In which setting is cardiac POCUS often used for serial monitoring of stable patients with chronic heart disease?
items.explanation: Cardiac POCUS is often used in outpatient settings for serial monitoring of stable patients with chronic heart disease.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · CARDIAC | LUNG AND PLEURA

items.question: Which condition can be detected by cardiac POCUS that may necessitate immediate management changes?
items.explanation: Acute right ventricular failure is a condition detectable by cardiac POCUS that can change immediate management.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · CARDIAC | LUNG AND PLEURA

items.question: What type of probe is used to penetrate deeper structures in the thorax for lung and pleural ultrasound?
items.explanation: Low-frequency probes are used to penetrate deeper structures in the thorax for lung and pleural ultrasound.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · CARDIAC | LUNG AND PLEURA

items.question: What is the minimum volume of peritoneal free fluid that POCUS can detect?
items.explanation: POCUS can detect as little as 100–500 mL of peritoneal free fluid.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · ABDOMEN

items.question: Which imaging modality is more reliable than automated bladder scanners for detecting urinary retention?
items.explanation: Bladder ultrasound (a form of POCUS) is more reliable than automated bladder scanners for urinary retention.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · ABDOMEN

items.question: What diagnostic criteria include the appearance of plicae circularis at the perimeter in small-bowel obstruction evaluation by POCUS?
items.explanation: The appearance of plicae circularis at the perimeter is part of the criteria for fluid-filled small-bowel loops.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · ABDOMEN

items.subtopic: LOWER EXTREMITY DEEP-VEIN THROMBOSIS | SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE | VASCULAR ACCESS
items.question: Which of the following is a key requirement for performing compression ultrasound to diagnose deep-vein thrombosis (DVT)?
options: A perpendicular compression technique ensuring complete venous compression with wall-to-wall touching.
Visualization of intraluminal clot within the vein.
Use of triplex ultrasound exams over duplex ultrasound exams.
Compression at random points along the veins.
items.explanation: A perpendicular compression technique is required to ensure complete venous compression with wall-to-wall touching for diagnosing DVT.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · LOWER EXTREMITY DEEP-VEIN THROMBOSIS | SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE | VASCULAR ACCESS

items.subtopic: LOWER EXTREMITY DEEP-VEIN THROMBOSIS | SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE | VASCULAR ACCESS
items.question: What does a noncompressible vein indicate during compression ultrasound for DVT diagnosis?
options: Need for further imaging with MRI.
items.explanation: A noncompressible vein is diagnostic of DVT, even without visualization of intraluminal clot.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · LOWER EXTREMITY DEEP-VEIN THROMBOSIS | SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE | VASCULAR ACCESS

items.subtopic: LOWER EXTREMITY DEEP-VEIN THROMBOSIS | SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE | VASCULAR ACCESS
items.question: What can point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS) rapidly differentiate between in skin and soft tissue infections?
items.explanation: POCUS allows rapid differentiation between reactive lymph nodes and abscesses among other conditions in skin and soft tissue infections.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · LOWER EXTREMITY DEEP-VEIN THROMBOSIS | SKIN AND SOFT TISSUE | VASCULAR ACCESS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement for competence in POCUS according to the passage?
options: Mastery of ultrasound knowledge including clinical indications, applications, limitations, and artifacts.
Image acquisition skills learned at varying rates with deliberate practice.
Board certification in internal medicine implying competence in POCUS.
Clinical integration of POCUS into patient care.
items.explanation: The passage states that board certification in internal medicine does not imply competence in POCUS.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · PATHWAYS | CERTIFICATION | CREDENTIALING AND PRIVILEGES | FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.subtopic: PATHWAYS | CERTIFICATION | CREDENTIALING AND PRIVILEGES | FUTURE DIRECTIONS
items.question: What is a potential future direction for POCUS use mentioned in the passage?
options: Increased use of tele-ultrasound for training and remote patient care.
Elimination of artificial intelligence from clinical practice.
Reduction in the portability of ultrasound devices.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that tele-ultrasound has shown promise for training clinicians and delivering patient care remotely as a future direction.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · PATHWAYS | CERTIFICATION | CREDENTIALING AND PRIVILEGES | FUTURE DIRECTIONS

items.explanation: The passage states that emergency medicine privileges include POCUS use as a core privilege.

items.statement: The goal of POCUS is to replace high-resolution imaging modalities like CT or MRI.

items.statement: Portable ultrasound machines can offer color flow and spectral Doppler imaging.
items.explanation: Portable devices provide two-dimensional imaging, as well as color flow and spectral Doppler imaging.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · Point-of-Care Ultrasound | DEFINITION

items.statement: Ultrasound is superior to chest x-ray for detecting small pleural effusions.
items.explanation: Ultrasound is indeed superior to chest x-ray for the detection of small pleural effusions.
items.reference: Point of Care Ultrasound · CARDIAC | LUNG AND PLEURA

items.statement: B-lines on lung ultrasound are indicative of normal aeration.
items.explanation: B-lines indicate interstitial abnormalities, not normal aeration, and can be seen in conditions like pulmonary edema.

items.statement: POCUS can specify the type of peritoneal free fluid.
items.explanation: POCUS cannot specify the type of peritoneal free fluid.

items.explanation: Bladder ultrasound is more reliable than automated bladder scanners for urinary retention.

items.assertion: Competence in basic cardiac POCUS is mandatory for emergency medicine.
items.reason: Cardiac POCUS guides immediate clinical decision-making and categorizes shock states.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why competence in cardiac POCUS is mandatory.

items.assertion: Lung sliding on ultrasound indicates a pneumothorax.
items.reason: Lung sliding results from respirophasic movement of the pleural interface.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; lung sliding indicates normal aeration, not pneumothorax. The reason is true.
